findings.claim: Session lifetime jumps 24x with no security boundary besides the token itself — a product request is not an auth policy decision, and this diff ships no compensating control (refresh token, re-auth, activity timeout).
The in-memory sessions map grows unbounded — expired sessions are only cleaned up on access. With 24h lifetimes and no reaper, abandoned sessions leak until the process restarts.

24x TTL increase with an in-memory Map and no background reaper turns every session into a 24-hour memory reservation. At 1h TTL, peak memory was bounded by one hour's concurrent users; at 24h, it's bounded by an entire day's cumulative logins. The process will OOM and restart — losing all sessions.

There is no revocation path. If a token is compromised, there is no invalidateSession or invalidateAllSessions(userId) — the only way to kill a token is to restart the process (losing all sessions) or wait 24 hours for it to expire naturally.

The comment 'per product request' is the sole justification for a 24x increase in token exposure window, but the diff assumes that a product request constitutes sufficient authority to change security posture. Who approved this as a security decision, not just a UX decision?

The new expiry-check in validateSession assumes that server-side expiry enforcement is the only control needed for a 24h window — it never defends the premise that no other session hygiene (rotation, revocation on privilege change, binding to IP/fingerprint) is required when tokens live 24x longer.

Session token lifetime increased 24x (from 1h to 24h) with no compensating controls — no refresh token rotation, no re-authentication on privilege change, no IP binding. A stolen token is now exploitable for 24 hours instead of 1 hour. The comment 'per product request' is not a security review.

There is no session revocation mechanism in this diff. If a user reports a compromised account, the only remediation is to wait up to 24 hours for natural expiry — or restart the process (destroying all sessions). No invalidateSession, no logout-all, no admin kill switch.
